questions.prompt: The transfer function of a series RLC circuit (input = voltage, output = current) is:
questions.explanation: H(s) = I(s)/V(s) = 1/Z_total = 1/(R+Ls+1/Cs). Multiplying numerator and denominator by Cs: H(s) = Cs/(LCs²+RCs+1). The denominator is a second-order polynomial in s.

questions.prompt: The denominator of the RLC transfer function determines:
options: The poles, natural frequency, and damping
questions.correctAnswer: The poles, natural frequency, and damping
questions.explanation: The denominator s²+(R/L)s+1/(LC)=0 gives the poles. The poles determine: natural frequency ωₙ=1/√(LC), damping ratio ζ=(R/2)√(C/L), and whether the response is overdamped, critically damped, or underdamped.

questions.prompt: The natural frequency ωₙ of a series RLC circuit is:
questions.explanation: ωₙ = 1/√(LC) is the frequency at which the circuit would oscillate with zero resistance. It depends only on the energy storage elements L and C, not on R (which controls damping, not frequency).

questions.prompt: The damping ratio ζ of a series RLC circuit is:
questions.explanation: ζ = (R/2)√(C/L) determines the type of response. It's proportional to R: more resistance = more damping. The factor √(C/L) comes from the ratio of energy storage elements.
When ζ<1, the discriminant is negative, giving complex conjugate poles: s = −ζωₙ ± jωₙ√(1−ζ²). The imaginary part means oscillation; the negative real part means the oscillation decays.

questions.prompt: An RLC circuit is critically damped when:
questions.explanation: Critical damping (ζ=1) occurs when R = 2√(L/C). This is the boundary between oscillatory and non-oscillatory response. The system returns to equilibrium as fast as possible without overshooting.

questions.prompt: If R = 0 in an RLC circuit, the system:
questions.explanation: With R=0, ζ=0. The poles are purely imaginary: s = ±jωₙ. The response is x(t)=A·cos(ωₙt+φ) — undamped oscillation at the natural frequency. No energy is dissipated, so oscillation continues indefinitely (ideal LC oscillator).

questions.prompt: The impedance of an inductor L in the s-domain is:
questions.explanation: From v=L(di/dt), taking the Laplace transform: V(s)=LsI(s), so Z_L=V/I=Ls. The impedance is proportional to frequency — inductors oppose changes in current more at higher frequencies.

questions.prompt: Adding more resistance R to an underdamped RLC circuit:
questions.explanation: Increasing R increases ζ=(R/2)√(C/L). More damping means oscillations decay faster. Eventually, if R>2√(L/C), the system becomes overdamped and stops oscillating entirely.

questions.prompt: An RLC circuit has two poles because it has:
options: Two energy storage elements (L and C)
questions.correctAnswer: Two energy storage elements (L and C)
questions.explanation: Each independent energy storage element (capacitor or inductor) adds one to the system order. With both L and C, the system is second-order with two poles. An RC or RL circuit (one storage element) is first-order with one pole.

questions.prompt: The total impedance of R, L, C in series is:
questions.explanation: Series impedances add: Z = Z_R + Z_L + Z_C = R + Ls + 1/(Cs). This is the direct s-domain equivalent of the time-domain KVL equation.

questions.prompt: Dividing numerator and denominator of H(s) = Cs/(LCs²+RCs+1) by LC gives:
questions.explanation: Divide top and bottom by LC: numerator Cs/LC = s/L. Denominator: LCs²/LC + RCs/LC + 1/LC = s² + (R/L)s + 1/(LC). This standard form directly reveals ωₙ²=1/(LC) and 2ζωₙ=R/L.
